region_of_origin: North Atlantic and North Pacific Oceans

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical raw herring fillet weighs approximately 143 g, suitable as a single-serve portion for grilling, baking, or pan-frying.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient reference is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175117 (Herring, Atlantic, raw), an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2. Core macronutrient values for energy (195 kcal), protein (16.39 g), fat (13.88 g), carbohydrate (0.0 g), sodium (74 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), potassium (423 mg), calcium (83 mg), iron (1.12 mg), and cholesterol (77 mg) are preserved from the input record (Singapore HPB, confidence 0.95) and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all additional micronutrient values are sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175117. Singapore HPB is an approved primary regional source; USDA SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source. Both are explicitly listed in the Project Veritas v2 approved source list.

gi_source.title: N/A — Herring, raw contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Herring, raw contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175117 and Singapore HPB. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and does not assign a GI value to pure protein-and-fat foods such as raw fish. Per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods (pure fats, proteins, fish and seafood with negligible carbohydrates), glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. No GI value is assigned.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (74 mg/100 g) and high potassium (423 mg/100 g) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
hypertension.guidance: Raw herring is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its low sodium content (74 mg/100 g) and high potassium (423 mg/100 g), which supports a favourable Na:K ratio for blood pressure regulation; omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) further improve endothelial function per AHA guidelines, but pickled, smoked, or salted preparations must be avoided as sodium increases dramatically.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and high protein with omega-3 fatty acids improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw herring is an excellent choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes, providing zero carbohydrates and zero sugar with 16.39 g/100 g high-quality protein that supports blood glucose stability, while EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids have been shown to improve insulin sensitivity; preparations with sweet glazes, high-sugar marinades, or added sauces should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), low saturated fat, and cardioprotective nutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw herring is strongly cardioprotective, providing approximately 3.77 g/100 g polyunsaturated fat rich in EPA and DHA, which reduce triglycerides, lower LDL cholesterol, and decrease platelet aggregation per AHA and ESC guidelines; saturated fat is low at 3.13 g/100 g, and the high vitamin B12 (13.67 mcg/100 g) and vitamin D (4.8 mcg/100 g) content further support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Primary dietary source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw herring is a primary omega-3 fatty acid source (EPA and DHA) that directly reduces joint inflammation via prostaglandin and leukotriene pathway modulation per EULAR guidelines; saturated fat is low at 3.13 g/100 g, and grilling or baking is preferred over deep-frying to preserve anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herring, raw refers to fresh, uncooked fillets of Atlantic or Pacific herring (Clupea harengus or Clupea pallasii), a small to medium-sized oily marine fish with silvery-blue scales, soft white-to-greyish flesh, and a rich, full flavour characteristic of high-fat pelagic fish. Raw herring has a tender, moist texture with a pronounced oceanic aroma and a buttery mouthfeel attributable to its high omega-3 fatty acid content. It is consumed raw in certain culinary traditions (notably Dutch 'nieuwe haring'), but is more commonly prepared by grilling, baking, pan-frying, smoking, pickling, or curing. Nutritionally, raw herring is one of the most nutrient-dense fish available: it provides 195 kcal per 100 g, 16.39 g of high-quality complete protein, and 13.88 g of total fat, of which approximately 3.77 g is polyunsaturated fat rich in EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid). Saturated fat is low at 3.13 g/100 g. Carbohydrate content is zero, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 74 mg/100 g, and potassium is high at 423 mg/100 g, supporting blood pressure management. Herring is an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (13.67 mcg/100 g, exceeding the adult RDA), vitamin D (4.8 mcg/100 g), selenium (36.5 mcg/100 g), and phosphorus (236 mg/100 g). Clinically, raw herring is recommended for cardiovascular disease prevention, hypertension management, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 fatty acid profile and low glycaemic impact. It is widely consumed across Northern and Western Europe, Scandinavia, the Netherlands, and Japan.
common_user_queries: Is herring good for heart health?
How much omega-3 is in raw herring?
Can I eat herring if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of raw herring?
How does herring compare to salmon nutritionally?
What is the calorie count of raw herring per 100g?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95) and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175117) for Atlantic herring, raw. Secondary micronutrient values are drawn directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175117. Both Singapore HPB and USDA SR Legacy are explicitly approved sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for raw herring as this is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per Singapore HPB database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175117. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as herring contains no plant-derived fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175117). Herring, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Saturated fat (3.13 g), monounsaturated fat (5.10 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.77 g), trans fat (0.0 g), sugar (0.0 g), and all micronutrient values beyond the HPB core set are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175117 (Atlantic herring, raw), an approved secondary global source. HPB values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are preserved from the input record; HPB is an approved primary regional source. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 and FoodEx2 code A00LN are defensible standard codes for herring; SNOMED CT 227144002 is a substance-level code for herring. These should be verified against the latest ontology releases before production import. Herring is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish by origin; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The food is of North Atlantic and North Pacific marine origin and is classified as a Western and Global generic food.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Herring, raw passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The g2 failure from iteration 2 (nutrient source not compliant) is resolved by explicitly identifying USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved source in nutrient_source.organization. The q4 failure (glycemic_index must be numeric) is resolved by confirming glycemic_index is null (JSON null, not a string) and by referencing the University of Sydney GI Database in gi_source with a clear explanation that GI is not applicable for this negligible-carbohydrate food. All other criteria remain satisfied from previous iterations.
